title: Lemonade Change
description:
Lemonade Change

At a lemonade stand, each lemonade costs $5. Customers are standing in a queue to buy from you and order one at a time (in the order specified by bills). Each customer will only buy one lemonade and pay with either a $5, $10, or $20 bill. You must provide the correct change to each customer so that the net transaction is that the customer pays $5.

Note that you do not have any change in hand at first.

Given an integer array bills where bills[i] is the bill the ith customer pays, return true if you can provide every customer with the correct change, or false otherwise.

Examples

  • Input: bills = [5,5,5,10,20]
    Output: true
    Explanation: From the first 3 customers, we collect three $5 bills in order. From the fourth customer, we collect a $10 bill and give back a $5. From the fifth customer, we give a $10 bill and a $5 bill. Since all customers got correct change, we output true.
  • Input: bills = [5,5,10,10,20]
    Output: false
    Explanation: From the first two customers in order, we collect two $5 bills. For the next two customers in order, we collect a $10 bill and give back a $5 bill. For the last customer, we can not give the change of $15 back because we only have two $10 bills. Since not every customer received the correct change, the answer is false.

Constraints

  • 1 <= bills.length <= 10^5
  • bills[i] is either 5, 10, or 20
solution_text:
Lemonade Change

Algorithm:

  1. Keep running counters of $5 and $10 bills on hand (we never give $20 as change)
  2. For a $5 customer: just take the bill
  3. For a $10 customer: need one $5 back. Fail if we don't have one
  4. For a $20 customer: need $15 back. Prefer giving one $10 + one $5 over three $5 bills
  5. If we can't make change for any customer, return false

Time Complexity: O(n) — single pass over bills

Space Complexity: O(1) — only two counters

Key Insights:

  • Greedy choice on the $20 customer: use a $10 + $5 first, saving the $5s for future $10 customers who must be paid in $5
  • Hoarding $5s is always at least as good as any alternative
